candidates.flash_summary: Six-year PBV insider who built and currently administers the full comp and benefits stack for 7,000+ employees. Knows every system, every vendor, and every quirk in the route driver incentive model — but has never led a function or managed direct reports.
key_strengths: End-to-end ownership of PBV's current comp and benefits programs across all employee groups
Deep knowledge of DSR incentive structure and frontline hourly pay practices
Strong HRIS fluency — primary Workday HCM admin for compensation module

scope_match.candidate_actual: Individual contributor managing programs; no direct reports
scope_match.role_requirement: Director leading a team of 3–4 comp and benefits analysts

scope_match.candidate_actual: Owns and administers PBV's full comp and benefits stack
scope_match.role_requirement: Full ownership of total rewards strategy and execution

scope_match.candidate_actual: 6 years at single employer; limited external benchmarking exposure
scope_match.role_requirement: Ability to challenge existing programs against external market

scope_match.candidate_actual: Maintains and refines existing programs; limited greenfield design
scope_match.role_requirement: Redesign of route driver incentive structure is a Year 1 priority

edc_data.scope_seasoning: Katherine's institutional depth is unmatched — she knows PBV's workforce, systems, and operational realities better than any external candidate could. The central question is whether she can step into the leadership and strategic design mandate this role requires, or whether her strength in administration will limit her appetite to challenge and rebuild the programs she's spent six years maintaining.

key_criteria.name: Total Rewards Program Design
key_criteria.evidence: Katherine administers PBV's full compensation structure including grade bands, merit cycle, and annual bonus programme across all employee groups at Pepsi Bottling Ventures. She has made incremental adjustments to the pay structure in each of the last three years but has not led a full program redesign from the ground up.

key_criteria.name: Hourly & Route Sales Incentive Structure
key_criteria.evidence: She is the operational owner of PBV's DSR commission model and manages the monthly incentive calculations for approximately 2,200 route sales representatives at Pepsi Bottling Ventures. Has deep working knowledge of the tier structure, payout mechanics, and the recurring disputes that arise from territory splits and product mix changes.

key_criteria.name: Benefits Administration & Cost Management
key_criteria.evidence: Manages annual benefits renewal and broker relationship with Mercer at Pepsi Bottling Ventures, coordinating across medical, dental, vision, and voluntary benefits for 7,100 employees. Negotiated a 4% reduction in medical plan cost increase in the most recent renewal cycle through plan design changes including high-deductible option introduction.

key_criteria.name: Compensation Analytics & HRIS
key_criteria.evidence: Serves as the primary Workday HCM compensation module administrator at Pepsi Bottling Ventures, managing configuration, testing, and training. Builds the annual comp review dashboards and has developed equity analysis tooling used by HR Business Partners during the merit cycle.

key_criteria.name: HR Business Partnering & Change Leadership
key_criteria.evidence: Partners with VP-level operations leaders across 12 facilities at Pepsi Bottling Ventures to communicate pay changes and resolve compensation disputes. Has not led a change management programme or directed a cross-functional team; her partnering has been primarily transactional and advisory rather than strategic.

compensation.flexibility: Open to discussion; primary motivation is scope and title rather than compensation step-up

why_interested.headline: Ready to own the function she's built
why_interested.detail: Six years of execution without the title — Katherine wants accountability and decision rights that match the work she's already doing.

why_interested.headline: PBV's growth as a real opportunity to build
why_interested.detail: PBV's ongoing facility expansion and workforce growth mean the Director role will have genuine scope to grow, not just maintain.

potential_concerns.concern: No direct reports experience — stepping from individual contributor to managing a 3–4 person team is a meaningful leadership leap, particularly while simultaneously inheriting a DSR incentive redesign in Year 1
Six unbroken years at one employer creates real risk of a purely internal frame of reference — may replicate existing programs rather than benchmark and challenge them
Limited greenfield program design experience; her track record is maintenance and optimisation of inherited structures rather than building compensation frameworks from a blank sheet

our_take.text: We believe Katherine is the closest thing to a ready-made fit PBV is likely to find — she knows the workforce, the systems, and the operational quirks better than any external hire could in their first 18 months. The risk is that she has never led people or been required to challenge the programmes she helped build. The Year 1 priority — redesigning the DSR incentive structure — will test whether she can step back from her own work and approach it with external rigour. Worth a direct conversation with the client about whether they want proven internal continuity with a leadership development investment, or external challenge with a longer ramp.
discussion_points: Has she ever challenged or proposed to scrap a programme she owned? What happened?
How does she plan to approach the DSR incentive redesign given she built the current model?
What's her view on where PBV's comp and benefits stack compares to market — can she be specific?
our_take.ai_rationale: Framed the Our Take around the core tension between institutional depth and the fresh perspective this role may need. Avoided an advance/hold recommendation per product principles — left the judgment to the client.

edc_data.search_name: Director of Compensation & Benefits · Pepsi Bottling Ventures

candidates.flash_summary: Experienced HR Director from PBV's closest competitor with direct ownership of a 4,800-person route sales and warehouse workforce. Strong operational partnership instincts and proven change leadership, but compensation design is not his primary depth.
key_strengths: Leads full HR function for 4,800-person Carolinas territory at the largest independent Coca-Cola bottler in the US
Direct experience with route driver pay and DSR incentive administration in the same DSD model as PBV
Track record of leading HR transformation including ATS implementation and manager effectiveness programme

scope_match.candidate_actual: Broad HR Director; comp and benefits is one of several accountabilities
scope_match.role_requirement: Pure total rewards specialist — comp design, benefits strategy, and analytics

scope_match.candidate_actual: 4,800-person DSD workforce at Coca-Cola Consolidated including route drivers, warehouse, and sales
scope_match.role_requirement: 7,000+ person DSD workforce; identical operational model to PBV

scope_match.candidate_actual: Leads team of 7 HR professionals across Carolinas region
scope_match.role_requirement: Director managing 3–4 comp and benefits analysts

scope_match.candidate_actual: Manages benefits administration but does not lead broker negotiations or plan design
scope_match.role_requirement: Full ownership of benefits vendor relationships and cost management strategy

scope_match.candidate_actual: Administers CCBCC's established comp bands; has not redesigned incentive structures
scope_match.role_requirement: DSR incentive structure redesign is a Year 1 deliverable

edc_data.scope_seasoning: James brings the industry credibility and operational partnership skills that are genuinely hard to find — he has lived the DSD workforce comp challenges from the other side of the competitive fence. The question is whether his generalist HR breadth substitutes for specialist total rewards depth, particularly given that the Year 1 mandate is a ground-up redesign of the route driver incentive structure rather than steady-state administration.

key_criteria.name: Total Rewards Program Design
key_criteria.evidence: James administers CCBCC's established compensation bands and merit cycle as part of a broader HR Director mandate but has not led compensation programme design. His comp knowledge has been applied primarily through execution of centrally-designed frameworks from CCBCC's corporate total rewards team rather than building structures independently.

key_criteria.name: Hourly & Route Sales Incentive Structure
key_criteria.evidence: Manages DSR pay and commission processing for 2,100 route sales representatives across the Carolinas region at Coca-Cola Consolidated, dealing with the same territory split disputes, product mix challenges, and seasonal variance issues PBV faces. Described the current CCBCC incentive model as "overdue for a redesign" — suggesting he has formed views on its weaknesses.

key_criteria.name: Benefits Administration & Cost Management
key_criteria.evidence: Responsible for regional benefits administration at Coca-Cola Consolidated but works within centrally-negotiated plan designs and vendor contracts. Has not personally led a benefits renewal, RFP, or broker selection process — these are managed at CCBCC's corporate headquarters in Charlotte with regional HR Directors as stakeholders rather than owners.

key_criteria.name: Compensation Analytics & HRIS
key_criteria.evidence: Proficient in Workday HCM at Coca-Cola Consolidated and has built regional comp dashboards used for headcount planning and manager effectiveness review. Describes himself as "comfortable with data but not a data person" — suggests functional proficiency rather than analytical leadership.

key_criteria.name: HR Business Partnering & Change Leadership
key_criteria.evidence: Led two significant change initiatives at Coca-Cola Consolidated: a manager effectiveness programme reaching 340 people managers across the Carolinas, and the regional rollout of the Greenhouse ATS replacing a legacy paper-based process. Both required sustained cross-functional engagement with operations, finance, and the VP-level leadership team.

compensation.flexibility: Acknowledged current total comp is above the likely market for a specialist Director role; willing to have a 'real conversation' once scope is confirmed

why_interested.headline: Specialisation after years of breadth
why_interested.detail: Wants to go deep in total rewards rather than continuing as a generalist — sees this as the natural next step for his career trajectory.

why_interested.headline: Limited ceiling at CCBCC
why_interested.detail: Corporate total rewards is centralised in Charlotte; regional HR Directors have minimal influence over comp and benefits strategy. He wants ownership he cannot get within the current CCBCC structure.

potential_concerns.concern: Compensation gap — current total comp of $171K is materially above the PBV Director budget ceiling; will require a frank discussion about the trade-off between scope gain and comp step-down

Never led a specialist comp function — his depth is generalist HR leadership with comp as one component, not the primary discipline. The Year 1 DSR incentive redesign requires technical compensation expertise he may not possess
Benefits ownership has always been centrally driven at CCBCC — he will be entering vendor negotiations, broker management, and plan design territory where he has limited hands-on experience

our_take.text: We believe James brings something genuinely rare: a person who has operated inside the same DSD workforce model, at the same scale, at PBV's nearest competitor. His instincts for the operational realities of route driver pay are real and validated. The concern is that he is a skilled HR generalist applying for a specialist role, and the comp gap — $171K current total versus a sub-$170K PBV ceiling — means this conversation will require an honest trade-off discussion. If he is genuinely motivated by the specialisation opportunity and willing to absorb a short-term comp reset, the industry knowledge could outweigh the technical depth question. Worth exploring whether his appetite for the DSR redesign is genuine ambition or surface-level interest.
discussion_points: Can he articulate what he would do differently with the DSR incentive structure — does he have a real hypothesis or a general observation?
Is the comp gap genuinely workable, or is he testing the market? A direct question is appropriate.
How has he developed compensation decisions in his current role when the central team owned the framework?
our_take.ai_rationale: Structured around the two dominant tensions: industry knowledge as a genuine differentiator versus generalist background as a real risk, and comp gap as a concrete practical concern. Did not recommend advance or hold per product principles.

The PBV Director role carries full ownership of benefits strategy and vendor management alongside comp design. An internal benefits specialist partnership or a rapid-ramp plan would be needed to bridge this.

key_criteria.evidence: Not mentioned

key_criteria.evidence: Designed variable pay structures for 6,000 hourly distribution workers at Reyes Beverage Group including warehouse, delivery driver, and merchandiser populations. Has modelled multiple incentive scenarios and led the transition from a legacy piece-rate structure to a performance-based variable model.

key_criteria.name: Benefits Administration & Cost Management
key_criteria.evidence: Not mentioned — Alicia's current mandate is compensation only. Benefits are owned by a separate team at Reyes Beverage Group.

key_criteria.name: Compensation Analytics & HRIS
key_criteria.evidence: Workday Advanced Compensation certified; primary comp module admin at Reyes Beverage Group. Described building a pay equity analysis framework from scratch that is now used quarterly.

key_criteria.name: HR Business Partnering & Change Leadership
key_criteria.evidence: Not mentioned

why_interested.headline: Seeking Director-level ownership
why_interested.detail: Has been a specialist for 8 years and wants a role with full function ownership including a team to develop.

potential_concerns.concern: No benefits ownership whatsoever — taking on a Director role with full benefits strategy accountability represents a major scope gap that will require a steep learning curve in Year 1

our_take.text: We believe Alicia's compensation credentials are the strongest in the slate on the technical side, and her beverage distribution background is genuinely relevant. The question the client needs to answer is how much benefits experience matters in Year 1 — if the DSR incentive redesign is the priority deliverable, she is arguably the best-equipped candidate to execute it. If benefits cost management is equally urgent, the gap is structural and would require active mitigation.
discussion_points: How important is benefits ownership depth in Year 1 versus compensation design capability?
What is her plan for developing benefits knowledge quickly if she were to join?
our_take.ai_rationale: Kept brief given this is a partial EDC. Focused on the core trade-off the client needs to make.

edc_data.scope_seasoning: Robert is the most credentialed candidate in the slate by a significant margin — but he is being asked to step down in both company scale and likely compensation. The genuine question is motivation: is the move to a smaller, privately-held bottler a considered career choice or a contingency position? His answer will determine whether this is worth progressing.

key_criteria.name: Total Rewards Program Design
key_criteria.evidence: Led the post-merger job architecture redesign at Keurig Dr Pepper covering 4,200 roles across the combined entity, establishing the grade structure and market pricing methodology now used across the enterprise. This represents the most significant greenfield design work in the current slate.

key_criteria.name: Hourly & Route Sales Incentive Structure
key_criteria.evidence: Not mentioned — KDP has a DSD network but Robert's mandate is enterprise-wide rather than workforce-segment specific. Route driver incentive design does not appear to be a primary area of focus in his current role.

key_criteria.name: Benefits Administration & Cost Management
key_criteria.evidence: Owns the full benefits strategy and vendor relationships for 26,000 KDP employees, including medical, dental, vision, life, disability, and voluntary. Achieved a 7% reduction in medical plan cost per employee over two consecutive renewal cycles through plan design changes and Rx formulary renegotiation.

key_criteria.name: Compensation Analytics & HRIS
key_criteria.evidence: Not mentioned

key_criteria.name: HR Business Partnering & Change Leadership
key_criteria.evidence: Not mentioned

compensation.flexibility: Not explored — compensation discussion was deferred pending confirmation of fit

why_interested.headline: Seeking autonomy over enterprise scale
why_interested.detail: Expressed a preference for a smaller organisation where he can see the direct impact of his decisions rather than being one layer in a large corporate function.

potential_concerns.concern: Compensation gap is likely material — KDP Director of Total Rewards at enterprise scale will be compensated significantly above PBV's budget ceiling. The conversation has not yet happened.

Motivation requires probing — moving from a 26,000-person CPG enterprise to a 7,000-person regional bottler is a meaningful scope reduction. Whether this is a genuine lifestyle/autonomy decision or a contingency needs establishing early.

our_take.text: We believe Robert is technically the strongest candidate in the slate — his credentials on job architecture, benefits cost reduction, and enterprise-scale ownership are directly relevant. The two unknown variables are compensation and motivation. Both need direct answers before this is worth progressing. The client should be comfortable asking both questions plainly at the next interaction.
discussion_points: What is his current total compensation, and what would he need to make the move viable?
What is specifically driving the interest in a smaller, private company — is this a genuine directional choice?
our_take.ai_rationale: Kept brief given partial EDC and the fact that the two dominant questions (comp and motivation) are unanswered. Avoided speculating on outcome.
